segments.s093-g65: کلاه کیانی is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian hat/crown' (noun followed by a post‑nominal adjective/epithet). به سر بر نهاد is a classical verbal phrase: نهاد is the past tense (3rd sg.) of نهادن 'to place', with the prefix/particle بر marking the 'upon' relation; به سر marks the location ('on the head'). The whole segment literally means 'he placed the Kayanian crown upon (his) head.'
segments.s093-g66: به دینار دادن uses دادن as an infinitive/gerund 'giving' and به with an instrumental sense 'by' (how it was done): 'by giving dinars.' دادن can also carry a causative feel here. در is the noun 'door/gate' and اندرگشاد is an adjective 'wide‑open' (poetic compound meaning the inner door open); the verb is implicit (caused to open), so the phrase means 'by giving dinars he caused the inner door to open.'
segments.s093-g25: بزد is the 3rd‑person past of زدن ('he struck'); Persian poetry here uses a verb‑first (V O) order for emphasis. گردن خسرو تاجدار is the object phrase 'the neck of Khosrow, the crowned (one)' with no written ezāfe: گردن (object) + proper name خسرو + epithet تاجدار in apposition.
segments.s093-g26: تنش را is the definite object ('his body', تن + enclitic ش = 'his', then object marker را). بخاک اندر افگند is a compound directional phrase (بِ + خاک + اندر = 'to/into the ground') + افگند, the past 'he threw/cast'. خوار is an adjectival comment after the verb meaning 'despised/lowly', qualifying the state in which the body was cast.
segments.s093-g27: شد is the past/coprial verb 'became/was' introducing the resulting state. آن یادگار منوچهر شاه is a noun phrase 'that remnant/memento of Manuchehr the king' (آن = demonstrative; یادگار = remnant/memento; منوچهر شاه in apposition). The verb precedes the noun phrase for a concise, poetic effect.
segments.s093-g28: تهی ماند is 'remained empty' (تهی = devoid/of, ماند = remained). ایران is the subject. ز introduces the thing it is deprived of (poetic form of از): 'empty of تخت و کلاه' (تخت = throne; کلاه literally 'hat' used metonymically here as 'crown'). The phrase follows standard S‑V order with ز marking the source/privation.
segments.s093-g29: ایا is a vocative/attention particle ('O' / 'indeed') here addressing someone. دانشی مرد is a compact epithet 'a man of knowledge' (دانشی modifies مرد without written ezāfe). بسیار هوش is an adjectival phrase 'of much/very great intellect' further qualifying the man; word order groups epithets before the admonition that follows.
segments.s093-g30: همه here functions adverbially 'altogether/wholly' or 'in no wise' before the object phrase. چادر آزمندی = 'the cloak/tent of greed' (چادر as cloak/veil metaphorically). مپوش is the negative present/imperative (م‑ prefix + پوش) meaning 'do not wear / do not cover (yourself with)'; the clause is a direct admonition to the addressee.
segments.s093-g31: که introduces a clause of reason/explanation ('for' / 'that'). تخت و کله 'throne and head' (positions of rulership) are listed, چون تو = 'like you'. بسیار دید is a past expression 'have often/ many have seen' or 'many have seen'; دید is past 'saw/beheld' with the implied subject 'many (people)' or generalizing sense. The clause means 'because many like you have experienced throne and leadership.'
segments.s093-g32: چنین داستان = 'such a story/tale' (چنین = 'such'), چند = 'a few / several', and خواهی شنید is a future construction: خواهی (auxiliary 'will') + شنید ('hear') giving 'you will hear'. Word order places the object phrase before the auxiliary+verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s093-g33: رسیدی is past tense, 2nd-person singular ('you arrived') with the subject omitted. به جایی is a prepositional phrase 'to/at a place'; که introduces a relative clause whose verb بشتافتی (past 2nd-person, 'you hastened'/'you rushed') comes at the end, so the clause reads 'the place that you had hurried to.'
segments.s093-g34: سرآمد here is a noun meaning 'outcome/goal' rather than 'foremost.' کزو is a fused prepositional pronoun 'from/of which,' linking سرآمد to آرزو ('desire, wish'). یافتی is past 2nd-person 'you found/obtained/attained,' so the whole phrase means 'the goal from which you obtained/attained your wish.' Note the verb closes the sentence as is typical in Persian.
segments.s093-g35: چه جویی is a rhetorical second-person present question 'what do you seek' (جوی‌/جویی here = 'seek'). از این تیره خاک نژند is a prepositional phrase 'from this ill-omened/bleak, base earth/dust' where تیره (ominous/bleak) and the postpositive adjective نژند ('base/worthless') modify خاک.
segments.s093-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which'); هم here means 'even/also' for emphasis; بازگرداندت is a third-person singular verb (بازگرداند) with the object clitic ت ('you') attached, 'returns/turns you back'; مستمند is the predicate adjective 'needy/poor.' The clause therefore reads 'that even returns you needy.'
segments.s093-g37: که‌گر is a fused conditional 'که اگر' = 'if.' چرخ گردان is a poetic compound 'the turning wheel' (a metaphor for fate). کشد is a third-person singular verb 'draws/pulls' governing the object 'تو' (you); زین ('from the saddle') indicates the source from which one is pulled. Word order and the conditional particle make this an if-clause describing a possible act of fate.
segments.s093-g38: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/at last.' خاکست بالین تو is a nominal phrase 'ashes [as] your pillow' with no overt copula (a common poetic/elliptical construction): 'in the end your pillow will be ashes.' بالین تو is a possessive phrase 'your pillow.'
segments.s093-g39: پس is a transitional 'then.' آن بستگان را is a definite object 'those relatives' marked by the object marker را. کشیدند is past 3rd-person plural and here means 'made (them) suffer/humiliated' (causative/resulting sense), and خوار is the predicate adjective 'despised/low,' so the clause reads 'then they made those relatives low/despised.'
segments.s093-g40: به جان is an idiomatic phrase here meaning 'for their lives' or 'earnestly' (indicating strong entreaty). خواستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they asked/desired' — here 'they begged.' آنگهی means 'then/thereupon,' and زینهار is an interjection/noun 'beware!/a warning.' Together: 'they begged for their lives and then cried, "Beware!"' or 'and then (came) the warning.'
segments.s093-g9: به دژخیم فرمود is a light-verb construction meaning 'he said/ordered to the torturer' (به + indirect object + فرمود). کو را is the marked object (را = definite/object marker) where کو functions here as a demonstrative/pronoun 'that one/him'; کشان is a present-participial adjective 'dragging' that modifies the object, so the whole segment supplies the addressee and what to do to the object.
segments.s093-g10: ببر is the imperative 'take! / carry off!' directed to the torturer. تا introduces a purposive/subsequent clause 'so that / in order that'. بیاموزد is a 3rd-person subjunctive meaning 'may learn / come to know' (here 'may learn a lesson'). او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he', and سرفشان is a descriptive epithet ('the head‑tosser' / proud fellow) applied to that person; it functions as the complement being referred to by او.
segments.s093-g11: سپهدار نوذر places the title (سپهدار) before the name (نوذر) as a nominal phrase 'Nowzar's commander' (or 'the commander, Nowzar'). چو آگاه شد is a temporal clause 'when he became aware'; چو = 'when' and شد is the past of شدن 'to become' used here in the sense 'became / came to be (aware)'.
segments.s093-g12: بدانست is the main past-tense verb 'he knew / he realized'. کش here functions as an archaic/poetic connective introducing the content clause (roughly 'that'); روز کوتاه شد is a subordinate clause 'the day grew short' with کوتاه شد = 'became short' describing that realization.
segments.s093-g13: سپاهی here denotes a host/army. پر از is the idiom 'full of' followed by the items that fill it: غلغل 'uproar/clamor' and گفت و گوی 'conversation/talk' (گفت + و + گوی form a paired noun meaning talk/discussion). The sequence lists the qualities of the host.
segments.s093-g14: سوی = 'toward(s)'; شاه نوذر names the addressee 'King Nowzar'. نهادند (past, 3rd pl.) + روی ('face') is an idiomatic combination here meaning 'they set/placed their face toward' i.e. 'they turned toward' — so the verb+noun together express 'turned their faces toward the king.'
segments.s093-g15: ببستند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they bound/tied'. بازوش is 'his arm' (بازو + enclitic -ش 'his'). با introduces the instrument 'with', بند تنگ is a noun phrase 'a tight fetter/band' (بند = fetter, تنگ = tight), so together 'they bound his arm with a tight fetter.'
segments.s093-g16: کشیدندش is the past 3rd-plural verb + object clitic 'they dragged/pulled him' (کشیدند + ـش). از جای = 'from the place' and پیش indicates 'in front of / before'; نهنگ here is a substantive used as an epithet 'the great/huge one' (poetic usage), so the clause says they dragged him out of his place before that great figure.
segments.s093-g57: ببخشید is a past 3rd-person plural verb here meaning 'they gave/bestowed' (not 'forgave'); جان‌شان = 'their lives' (جان with the clitic -شان). به + گفتار اوی = 'at/on his word' (به as a preposition meaning 'at' or 'upon', گفتار = 'speech/word', اوی is the archaic/poetic possessive 'his'). Word order (verb before object) and the enclitic -شان are typical of epic Persian.
segments.s093-g58: چو = 'when' (archaic/poetic conjunction). بشنید is 3rd-person singular past 'he heard'. با introduces an accompaniment or manner ('with'), and درد پیکار is a compound meaning 'painful/ grievous struggle' (درد here as emotional/suffering). اوی is again the possessive/third-person reference 'his'. The clause describes the subject's reaction upon hearing.
segments.s093-g59: بفرمودشان is the past-tense verb 'he commanded' with the object clitic -شان 'them'. تا here marks purpose ('so that' / 'in order that') and is followed by a subjunctive/purpose clause. به + ساری is a destination ('to Sari')—here ساری functions as a place name (poetic diction may omit marking), and برند is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-plural of بردن used after تا to indicate the intended action ('may they carry/take').
segments.s093-g60: This is a coordinated list of the manner/instruments: به غل و به مسمار و خواری برند = 'let them carry/lead (him) with fetters and with a stake and with humiliation.' Repeated به marks each item; غل and مسمار name physical instruments (fetter, peg/nail), خواری is an abstract noun 'humiliation/shame', and برند repeats the same verb as above (3rd-pl subjunctive).
segments.s093-g61: چو = 'when'. این کرده شد is a perfect/passive construction 'this having been done' (کرده as participle, شد as auxiliary 'was'). ساز رفتن گرفت is an idiomatic sequence: ساز = 'preparation/means', رفتن = infinitive 'to go', and گرفت = 'took' used inchoatively ('he took up/laid hands to') — together 'he began preparations for departure.'
segments.s093-g62: زمین زیر اسپان نهفتن گرفت is a verbless-style clause with گرفت giving an inchoative sense 'began to be/was taken to be': 'the ground (زمین) under (زیر) the horses (اسپان) began to be hidden/was hidden.' نهفتن is the infinitive 'to hide' used here in a passive/inchoative sense plus گرفت.
segments.s093-g63: ز پیش = 'from before/from the front of' (ز is the poetic form of از, پیش = 'front/before'). دهستان is the locality 'the district/village-territory'; سوی ری کشید = 'moved/advanced toward Rey' (سوی = 'toward', ری = the city Rey). کشید is a past tense verb 'he/they drew/marched' (poetic diction for 'moved/rode').
segments.s093-g64: از اسپان = 'from the horses' (here idiomatically 'on horseback' or 'with the horse contingent'). به رنج و به تک خوی = 'with hardship and with single (or characteristic) disposition' where به marks manner/instrument ('with') and تک خوی = 'single-mindedness / solitary disposition'. کشید again is the past verb 'they rode/proceeded'.
segments.s093-g17: به دشت آوریدندش: 'به' is the allative/prepositional particle 'to/into' and introduces the destination 'دشت' (the plain). آوریدندش is a past plural narrative form 'they brought' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him'. از خیمه خوار: 'از' = 'from/out of' and 'خیمه' = 'tent'; 'خوار' is an adjective meaning 'despised/humiliated' qualifying the person taken out.
segments.s093-g18: برهنه سر و پای و برگشته کار: adjective-before-noun order 'برهنه سر و پای' = 'with head and feet bare' (a stock epic phrase). 'برگشته کار' is a nominal phrase where برگشته 'turned/reversed' modifies کار 'affairs/situation', i.e. 'his affairs/fortune are overturned.'
segments.s093-g19: چو از دور دیدش زبان برگشاد: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'از دور' = 'from afar'; دیدش = 'he saw him' (دید + enclitic object -ش). 'زبان برگشاد' literally 'opened his tongue' — a fixed expression meaning 'he began to speak', where برگشاد (opened) modifies زبان.
segments.s093-g20: ز کین نیاگان همی کرد یاد: 'ز' (poetic variant of از) = 'of/from' and introduces the cause/content. 'کین نیاگان' = 'the hatred/vengeance of the ancestors' (کین = enmity/vengeance, نیاگان = forefathers). همی کرد یاد is a periphrastic construction: همی gives durative/aspectual flavor and کرد (light verb) + یاد (noun) = 'made remembrance' → 'recalled' or 'called to mind.'
segments.s093-g21: ز تور و ز سلم اندر آمد نخست: 'ز' = 'from/of' (poetic), repeated for each coordinated term 'تور و سلم' (here referring to the named figures/recipients). 'اندر آمد' = 'came in/entered' (past narrative), and 'نخست' = 'first' places this action first in sequence. Grammatically this says something (an impression, feeling, or news) first entered/affected Tur and Salm.
segments.s093-g22: دل و دیده از شرم شاهان بشست: 'دل و دیده' (heart and eye) are coordinated subjects. 'از شرم شاهان' = 'from the shame of the kings' (شرم = shame, شاهان = kings). بشست is past tense 'washed' — used here metaphorically/passively to mean 'were bathed/filled (with shame)' or 'were cleansed/wet by shame' (i.e. they felt shame).
segments.s093-g23: بدو گفت هر بد که آید سزاست: 'بدو' is the allative/clitic form 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'). گفت is past 'said' (narrative). The clause 'هر بد که آید سزاست' is a generalizing relative: 'every/bad (هر بد) that comes (که آید) is deserved (سزاست)' — i.e. 'whatever ill befalls him is deserved.'
segments.s093-g24: بگفت و برآشفت و شمشیر خواست: a simple sequence of actions in past tense: بگفت 'he said' (narrative), و برآشفت 'and he became angry/upset' (برآشفت = grew angry), و شمشیر خواست 'and he wanted/asked for the sword' (خواست = desired/requested; here meaning he reached for or demanded a sword).
segments.s093-g1: آگهی (the news/information) is the thing that 'arrived' (رسید) and is placed before the verb in this classical Persian order; سوی + noun marks the recipient/direction ('to the Turkish king'). رسید is simple past 'reached/arrived.'
segments.s093-g2: کزان is a relative/causal particle 'by/from which' introducing the result clause; نامداران جهان is the nominal phrase 'the renowned men of the world'; شد تهی is a copular past construction where شد 'became' takes تهی 'empty/bereft' as predicate, i.e. 'were left empty/the world was bereft of them.'
segments.s093-g3: دلش is the noun 'heart' + enclitic possessive 'his'; گشت is past 'became/turned'; پر آتش is a predicative phrase 'full of fire' (metaphorical 'burning'); از marks cause 'from/because of' and links to the causes درد و غم.
segments.s093-g4: دو رخ را is a definite direct object ('both cheeks/faces' + object marker را); به خون جگر is an instrumental/means phrase 'with the blood of the liver/heart (جگر as seat of feeling)'; داد نم literally 'gave moisture' = 'moistened/shed tears' (poetic expression meaning he wept intensely).
segments.s093-g5: برآشفت is simple past 'became angry/was enraged'; و گفتا 'and he said' introduces reported speech; که + interrogative کجا + ست forms an indirect question 'where is ...' with ست the archaic/copular form of است.
segments.s093-g6: کزو 'from whom' links the clause to the person sought; ویسه is the subject; خواهد is the future auxiliary 'will'; همی here is an emphatic/old preverb particle ('indeed' / marks the verbal construction); کینه خواست is a verbal phrase 'desire/seek vengeance' (کینه object, خواست 'desire' forming 'will desire/seek vengeance').
segments.s093-g7: چه چاره است is a rhetorical question 'what remedy/what else can be done'; جز 'except' introduces the only alternative; خون او ریختن is an infinitival clause 'to spill his blood' functioning as the complement of است (i.e. the suggested course of action).
segments.s093-g8: یکی here signals 'one (option)/one action' — an alternative course; کینه نو 'a new/fresh hatred or grudge' is the object; برانگیختن is the infinitive 'to arouse/rouse' and completes the alternative: 'to arouse a new grudge.'
segments.s093-g49: گرفتار (here a participial/nominal idea 'the captured' or 'being captured') + کشتن (verbal noun 'killing') is treated as the act/topic; نه والا بود is a negative predication: والا 'noble/lofty' is the predicate and بود (past copula) states it was not so. The clause thus says that the killing of the captured was not noble. Note the past copula بود marking a statement about how things stood.
segments.s093-g50: نشیبست is a nominal phrase 'a descent / lowland' functioning as the subject; جایی 'a place' follows as an apposition/locative. که introduces a relative clause 'that/where' and بالا بود 'was high' uses بالا as predicate with بود as copula in the past. The whole clause describes the place as a low hollow in contrast to its former height.
segments.s093-g51: سزد‌گر is an impersonal/conditional particle meaning 'if it befit / if it is proper'; نیاید is a negative subjunctive/present form 'should not come' (here 'should not befall'); به جانشان گزند literally 'harm to their lives' is the object of نیاید. Together the phrase means 'if it is fitting that no harm come to their lives.'
segments.s093-g52: سپاری is an imperative/optative or infinitival/imperative-like verb 'entrust / deliver'; همیدون is a proper name (the addressee or agent entrusted with them). به من شان ببند is an imperative clause: به من = 'to me', شان = enclitic 'their/them', ببند is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'bind / fasten / secure'. So the line orders: 'entrust to Hamidun and bind them to me.'
segments.s093-g53: بریشان is an older fused form meaning 'for them / upon them' (بر + ایشان); یکی 'one' modifies غار 'cave'; زندان 'prison' is appositive or descriptive noun; کنم is the 1st‑person singular subjunctive/intentional 'I will make / I will set up.' The clause thus expresses the speaker's resolve to make a single cave a prison for them.
segments.s093-g54: نگهدارشان is a compound noun 'their keepers/guardians' (نگهدار + شان), هوشمندان 'the wise ones' is the plural noun in apposition or the group to be appointed, and کنم again is 'I will make / I will appoint.' Grammatically this is a causative/result clause: 'I will make wise men their guards.'
segments.s093-g55: به ساری به زاری are two adverbial phrases ('by/with a flowing/song‑like (lament?) and by lamentation') giving the manner; برآرند is a 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'they will bring/restore' and هوش is the object 'sense/wits.' The structure means 'they will restore their wits by song and lamentation.' (These are poetic, somewhat idiomatic measures of consolation.)
segments.s093-g56: تو 'you' starts a direct address; از خون 'from/of blood' qualifies the following hand-image: به کش دست is an archaic phrasing meaning roughly 'with a hand that draws blood' or 'with a blood-stained hand' (کش 'draw/pull' here part of a compound image with دست). و چندین مکوش uses و 'and' + چندین 'many/several' + مکوش, an old negative imperative 'do not strive/attempt.' So the clause warns the addressee: 'you, with a blood-stained hand, do not attempt many things.'
segments.s093-g41: چو ... بدید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' introduces the time clause; اغریرث پرهنر is a noun phrase (proper name + adjective) meaning 'skillful Aghrirath'; آن is a demonstrative/object marker here ('that'/'it') placed before the verb in poetic order; بدید is the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s093-g42: دل او is the possessed noun 'his heart'; ببر is the noun 'tiger' describing the heart (a metaphorical apposition) and در = 'in/inside' locates that image within him; چو here = 'like/as' introduces a simile and دمید is a past verb (literally 'breathed'/'blew') referring back to the heart — the clause has a poetic, verbless linking between the noun phrase and the simile.
segments.s093-g43: همی گفت is a classical/narrative phrasing where همی gives an ongoing or iterative sense ('kept saying' / continuous past) and گفت would normally be the past verb 'said' (here the given lemma senses did not include 'said', so the precise verbal sense is the past of گفت). چندین سر بی‌گناه is the direct object phrase 'several innocent heads' (چندین = 'several', سر = 'head', بی‌گناه = 'innocent').
segments.s093-g44: ز تن دور ماند is a passive/result-like phrasing: ز = 'from', تن = 'body', دور ماند literally 'remained distant' (= 'were separated / taken away'); به فرمان شاه marks the agent/instrument by which that happened: به + فرمان = 'by command', شاه = 'the king'.
segments.s093-g45: بیامد is a past verb 'came'; خروشان is an adverbial/participial modifier 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the manner of coming; به خواهشگری gives purpose with به ('to/for') + خواهشگری = 'entreaty/pleading' — together 'came roaring to plead'.
segments.s093-g46: بیاراست here functions as a predicative/adjectival verb form meaning 'arrayed' / 'well‑adorned' (poetic use allows elision of an explicit copula: 'he came ... arrayed'); با introduces accompaniment or means ('with'), نامور = 'renowned', and داوری = 'judgment' (i.e. 'with renowned justice' or 'bearing famed justice').
segments.s093-g47: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'); چندین = 'several'; سرافراز describes those men as 'proud/honored/triumphant'; گرد here is a poetically used noun meaning a group/troop (archaic usage — not the common senses listed), and و سوار = 'and horsemen/riders' — together 'several proud troops and horsemen.'
segments.s093-g48: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'; با + ترگ و جوشن = 'with rags and cuirass/armor' (ترگ = 'rag/shred', جوشن = 'armor') — here نه با ... denies accompaniment/attire; نه در کارزار = 'nor in the battlefield' with در marking location. The two negative phrases are parallel, linked by نه ... نه.

sentences.s093-s33: He placed the Kayanian crown upon his head; by giving dinars he caused the inner door to open.
sentences.s093-s13: He struck the neck of Khosrow the crown‑bearer; he threw his body to the ground, humiliated.
sentences.s093-s14: That remnant of King Manuchehr was gone; Iran remained empty of throne and crown.
sentences.s093-s15: O man of learning and great wit, do not clothe yourself in the cloak of greed.
sentences.s093-s16: For many like you have seen throne and head; you will hear several such tales.
sentences.s093-s17: You reached the place to which you had hurried, and you attained the goal you longed for.
sentences.s093-s18: What are you seeking from this bleak, base dust that will even return you needy?
sentences.s093-s19: If the turning wheel should drag you from your saddle, in the end your pillow will be ashes.
sentences.s093-s20: Then those relatives were brought low; they begged for their lives and afterwards cried, 'Beware!'
sentences.s093-s5: He ordered the torturer, 'Drag him off, so that he — the head-tosser — may learn his lesson.'
sentences.s093-s6: When Nowzar's commander became aware, he realized that the day had grown short.
sentences.s093-s7: An army, full of clamor and talk, turned its face toward King Nowzar.
sentences.s093-s8: They bound his arm with a tight band and dragged him from his place before the great one.
sentences.s093-s29: They gave up their lives at his word; when he heard it, he took up the fight in grievous sorrow.
sentences.s093-s30: He ordered them to take (him) to Sari; to lead him in fetters and on a stake, and to bring him in humiliation.
sentences.s093-s31: When this had been done, he took up preparations to go; the earth became hidden beneath the horses.
sentences.s093-s32: From before the district they drew toward Rey; from the horses (i.e. on horseback) they rode with hardship and with single-minded disposition.
sentences.s093-s9: They brought him out from the tent into the plain, humiliated, with head and feet bare and his fortunes overturned.
sentences.s093-s10: When he saw him from afar he opened his tongue; he recalled the hatred of his forefathers.
sentences.s093-s11: First it entered Tur and Salm; the hearts and eyes of the kings were washed with shame.
sentences.s093-s12: He said to him, 'Whatever evil comes is deserved.' He spoke, became angry, and demanded a sword.
sentences.s093-s1: News reached the Turkish king that the world had been emptied of its renowned men.
sentences.s093-s2: His heart became full of fire from pain and sorrow; he moistened both cheeks with the blood of his heart.
sentences.s093-s3: He grew angry and said, 'Where is Nowzar? — from whom Viseh will seek vengeance.'
sentences.s093-s4: What remedy is there except to spill his blood, or to arouse a fresh grudge?
sentences.s093-s25: It was not noble to slay the captured; it was a low hollow where once there had been high ground.
sentences.s093-s26: If it is fitting that no harm come to their lives, entrust them to Hamidun and bind them to me.
sentences.s093-s27: I will make for them one cave as a prison; I will set wise men to keep them.
sentences.s093-s28: By song and by lamentation they will recover their senses; you, with a hand stained in blood, do not attempt many deeds.
sentences.s093-s21: When he saw that skillful Aghrirath, his heart, like a tiger within him, breathed like fire.
sentences.s093-s22: He kept saying that many innocent heads had been separated from the bodies by the king’s command.
sentences.s093-s23: He came roaring to plead, arrayed with renowned justice.
sentences.s093-s24: who had several proud troops and horsemen, not with rags and cuirass, nor in the battlefield.
